content_en:
Another general principle with respect to business transactions is that they are permissible unless there is evidence to demonstrate that they are forbidden; only if it is found that it contains some forbidden aspect will it be deemed forbidden. Thus, Islamic Law has laid down some principle guidelines while delineating particular forbidden aspects that must be avoided. The matters that should be avoided include unstated or undetermined terms, speculative or overly risky conditions, interest, gambling and fraud or deception. If any one of these factors is found in a contract, the contract, depending on the extent to which they are present, may be rendered null, void and impermissible. It is essential that Muslims be aware of these forbidden characteristics so

325 Recorded by al-Bukhari and Muslim. 326 Recorded by al-Bukhari and Muslim. 201

that they may live off of pure and permissible sources. Thus, some of them are discussed in some detail below.

Gharar refers to speculative or overly risky transactions. Imam Muslim records in his Sahih: "On the authority of Abu Hurairah who said that the Messenger of Allah (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) forbade 'sales of speculative nature' (bai' al-gharar)." Al-Bukhari and Muslim record: "On the authority of ibn Umar who said that 'the Messenger of Allah (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) prohibited the sale of fruits until their ripeness and freedom from disease were apparent. He prohibited both the seller and the buyer.'" Commenting on a hadith with similar meaning, al-Nawawi explained why the prohibition was for both the seller and the buyer. He wrote, "As for the seller, it is because he is wanting to devour wealth wrongfully. As for the buyer, it is because he is in accord with him on this forbidden act and because he is [possibly] wasting his wealth while wasting wealth has been prohibited."327

From these hadith and others, there is a consensus among jurists that an overwhelming presence of undue risk or uncertainty renders a business contract null and void. Such transactions are ones in which the probability of one or both of the parties being wronged is great. Concerning the meaning of this concept of gharar, Rayner states,

The Sharee'ah determined that in the interests of fair, ethical dealing in commutative contracts, unjustified enrichment should be prohibited. This policy precludes any element of uncertainty or risk (Gharar).328 In a general context, the unanimous proposition of the jurists held that in any transaction, by failing or neglecting to define any of the essential pillars of contract relating to the consideration or the object, the parties undertake a risk which is not indis-

327 Yahya al-Nawawi, al-Minhaaj Sharh sahih Muslim ibn a-Hajjaaj (Beirut: Daar al-Ma'rifah, 1999), vol. 10, p. 424. 328 The statement, "any element of uncertainty or risk," is not quite correct. The Muslim jurists have long recognized that amounts of "uncertainty" or "risk" exist in many legal contracts. The true question, as much of Rayner's quote itself further shows, is the predominance or the effect of such "uncertainty" or "risk" on the essence of the contract. 202

pensable for them. This kind of risk was deemed unacceptable and tantamount to speculation due to its inherent uncertainty. Speculative transactions with these characteristics are therefore prohibited…329

Although such contracts are prohibited by Islamic Law, due to their speculative or risky nature and hence the possibility of making gains from such transactions, they can be very alluring to individuals.330 Thus, ibn al-Atheer, going back to the lexical meaning of the term, says, "Al-Gharar is that concerning which its apparent component is preferable but its non-apparent component is disliked to the person. Hence, its apparent component entices the buyer while its non-apparent component is unknown."331

According to ibn Juzay, examples of gharar transactions include:

(1) "Ignorance of the price and uncertainty about the existence of the object."332

(2) "Uncertainty about the price of the object and about its characteristics, as in the example of the sale of cloth in a shop without any specification about its quality or price."333

(3) "Uncertainty related to difficulties of delivery."334

(4) "Uncertainty about the existence of the object, as in the case of a sickly animal."335

329 S. E. Rayner, The Theory of Contracts in Islamic Law (London: Graham & Trotman, 1991), p. 289. 330 Both parties must enter into contacts out of free will. However, that does not mean that every contract in which parties are willing to enter is permissible. 331 Mubaarak ibn al-Atheer, Jaami al-Usool fi Ahaadeeth al-Rasool (Maktaba al-Hilwaani. 1971), vol. 1, pp. 527-528. 332 Nayla Comair-Obeid, The Law of Business Contracts in the Arab Middle East (London: Kluwer Law Interna onal, 1996), p. 58. 333 Ibid., p. 58. 334 Ibid., p. 58. 335 Ibid., p. 58. 203

The Aspect of Riba (Interest)

One of the well-known great sins is the taking or paying of riba (inter336 est). Indeed, any Muslim familiar with the numerous texts censuring riba would undoubtedly do his best to avoid any trace of riba. For example, Allah has said in the Quran, "Those who devour interest will not stand [on the Day of Judgment] save as he arises whom the devil has deranged by (his) touch. That is because they say, 'Trade is just like interest,' whereas Allah has permitted trading and has forbidden interest. He unto whom an admonition from his Lord comes, and (he) refrains (in obedience thereto), shall keep [the money of] that which is past, and his affair (henceforth) is with Allah. As for him who returns (to interest), such are rightful owners of the Fire. They will abide therein forever. Allah destroys interest and gives an increase for charity. Allah loves not every disbelieving, sinner. Truly, [as for] those who believe, perform righteous deeds, establish the prayer and pay the zakah, their reward is with their Lord. No fear shall come upon them neither shall they grieve. O you who believe! Observe your duty to Allah, and give up what remains (due to you) in interest, if you are (in truth) believers. And if you do not, then be informed of a war from Allah and His messenger. But if you repent, then you have your principal [without interest]. Do not wrong [others] and you shall not be wronged" (al-Baqarah 275-279).

Among the other numerous Quranic and hadith texts concerning interest is the following: Jaabir stated, "The Messenger of Allah (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) cursed the taker of interest, its giver, its recorder and its two witnesses. They are all alike."337 In this important hadith of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) one sees that the giver and the receiver as well as those who assisted in this forbidden contract are all equally sinful and have all been cursed by the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him).

336 The word riba is sometimes very poorly and improperly translated into English as "usury." "Usury" implies an exorbitant amount of interest, above and beyond what is permissible by law. In Islamic law, any increase above the principle is forbidden. Hence, any positive rate of interest, no matter how low, is both interest and usury in Islamic law. Hence, the word interest is a much better translation for the word riba. 337 Recorded by Muslim. 204

Thus, it is forbidden to pay interest or to receive interest, and this includes the interest that one earns on one's checking or savings account. In general, if an individual wishes to loan money to another individual who is in need, this act should be a brotherly and charitable act and there should be no thought of reaping monetary benefits in such a case. If someone wishes to make a "business loan," hoping to reap profits, then he should be willing to put his risk and not demand a guaranteed return for his money. It is not fair that the borrower is at risk in his business venture while the lender faces no risk at all.

At the same time, Islam opens the door to many avenues by which injustice is avoided while investment still takes place. Hence, profit-sharing agreements on business loans are allowed while fixed interest payments are not.

Fraud and deception are also forbidden in Islam. This would include intentionally concealing defects in one's merchandise or work. One time the Prophet (peace be upon him) was in the marketplace and he put his hand into a pile of grains and he found that it was wet on the bottom. He asked the vendor about it and the man told him that rain had fallen upon it. So the Prophet (peace be upon him) told him, "Why did you not put it on top of the grain so people could see it? Whoever deceives is not from me."338

Ibn Maajah records that the Prophet (peace be upon him) said in another hadith stressing the aspect of brotherhood and its relation to business dealings, "A Muslim is a brother to a Muslim. It is not permissible for a Muslim to sell something defective to his brother without making that [defect] clear to him."339

Other Forbidden Sources of Wealth

There are other obvious forbidden forms of obtaining wealth. These include robbery, through bribery, gambling and extremely speculative transac338 Recorded by Muslim. 339 Recorded by ibn Maajah. Ahmad and al-Haakim have something similar. According to alAlbaani, it is sahih. See al-Albaani, sahih al-Jaami, vol. 2, p. 1136. 205

tions. A Muslim is also not allowed to sell or trade articles that are forbidden in Islam, even if he himself does not consume them. Hence, a Muslim is not allowed to sell alcohol, porn, drugs and the like.

In this chapter, the interactions of a believer were discussed. The true belief in God should permeate every aspect of an individual's life, especially his interaction with others of God's creation. Thus, starting with how he treats himself, the individual realizes that he is one of God's creatures and therefore must treat himself in a way that is pleasing to God. In order to achieve this goal, Allah has mercifully sent humans clear guidance to teach them how to behave even with respect to their own selves.

The individual created by God obviously, then, cannot claim for himself the right to treat others in any way he wishes. Again, Allah has provided guidance in this area as well. Whether it is with respect to one's parents, children, neighbors, others in society-even animals and inanimate objects such as wealth-there is a way of behavior that is representative of one's belief in Allah. It behooves a believer to learn this proper behavior and to exert himself to live by it in his life to the best of his ability.

Fortifying and Increasing One's Faith

This chapter is a discussion of the means of fortifying and increasing one's faith. As has been alluded to earlier, a Muslim should never be complacent with respect to his faith. Faith increases and decreases. The Messenger of Allah (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) said, "By the One whom there is no other God, one of you does the actions of Paradise until there is just a hand's span between him and Paradise and then the Book [preordainment] overtakes him and he does the actions of the people of Hell and he enters into it."340

A Muslim must be very clear about his goal in life. When he is clear about his goal in life, he must be able to identify the means and measures that will assist him to meet that goal. Similarly, he must be aware of the impediments and harmful aspects that harm him with respect to his goal. Finally, when he does slip, he needs to know the best way that will put him, Allah willing, right back on the proper track.
content_id:
Prinsip umum lainnya dalam transaksi bisnis adalah bahwa semuanya diperbolehkan kecuali terdapat bukti yang menunjukkan bahwa transaksi tersebut dilarang; hanya jika ditemukan adanya unsur yang dilarang, barulah transaksi itu dianggap terlarang. Dengan demikian, hukum Islam telah menetapkan beberapa pedoman prinsip sekaligus merinci aspek-aspek terlarang yang harus dihindari. Hal-hal yang harus dihindari meliputi syarat yang tidak dinyatakan atau tidak ditentukan, kondisi yang spekulatif atau mengandung risiko berlebihan, riba, perjudian, serta penipuan atau kecurangan. Jika salah satu faktor ini ditemukan dalam suatu akad, maka akad tersebut — bergantung pada sejauh mana faktor itu hadir — dapat dinyatakan batal, tidak sah, dan tidak diperbolehkan. Sangat penting bagi umat Islam untuk mengetahui karakteristik-karakteristik yang dilarang ini agar

325 Diriwayatkan oleh al-Bukhari dan Muslim. 326 Diriwayatkan oleh al-Bukhari dan Muslim. 201

mereka dapat hidup dari sumber-sumber yang murni dan halal. Oleh karena itu, sebagian dari hal-hal tersebut dibahas secara lebih rinci di bawah ini.

Gharar merujuk pada transaksi yang bersifat spekulatif atau mengandung risiko yang berlebihan. Imam Muslim meriwayatkan dalam Shahih-nya: "Dari Abu Hurairah, ia berkata bahwa Rasulullah (shallallahu 'alaihi wa sallam) melarang 'jual beli yang bersifat spekulatif' (bai' al-gharar)." Al-Bukhari dan Muslim meriwayatkan: "Dari Ibnu Umar, ia berkata bahwa 'Rasulullah (shallallahu 'alaihi wa sallam) melarang penjualan buah-buahan hingga kematangannya dan kebebasannya dari penyakit tampak jelas. Beliau melarang baik penjual maupun pembeli.'" Mengomentari hadits dengan makna serupa, al-Nawawi menjelaskan mengapa larangan itu berlaku bagi keduanya. Beliau menulis, "Adapun penjual, karena ia ingin memakan harta secara batil. Adapun pembeli, karena ia bersekongkol dengannya dalam perbuatan terlarang ini dan karena ia [kemungkinan] menyia-nyiakan hartanya, sementara menyia-nyiakan harta telah dilarang."327

Dari hadits-hadits ini dan lainnya, terdapat ijmak di antara para fuqaha bahwa kehadiran risiko atau ketidakpastian yang berlebihan secara dominan menjadikan akad bisnis batal dan tidak sah. Transaksi semacam itu adalah transaksi di mana kemungkinan salah satu atau kedua pihak dirugikan sangat besar. Mengenai makna konsep gharar ini, Rayner menyatakan,

Syariah menetapkan bahwa demi kepentingan transaksi yang adil dan beretika dalam akad pertukaran, pengayaan yang tidak sah harus dilarang. Kebijakan ini meniadakan setiap unsur ketidakpastian atau risiko (Gharar).328 Dalam konteks umum, proposisi yang disepakati oleh para fuqaha menyatakan bahwa dalam setiap transaksi, dengan gagal atau mengabaikan pendefinisian salah satu rukun pokok akad yang berkaitan dengan imbalan atau objek, para pihak menanggung risiko yang tidak

327 Yahya al-Nawawi, al-Minhaaj Syarh Shahih Muslim ibn al-Hajjaj (Beirut: Dar al-Ma'rifah, 1999), jil. 10, hlm. 424. 328 Pernyataan "setiap unsur ketidakpastian atau risiko" tidaklah sepenuhnya tepat. Para fuqaha Muslim telah lama mengakui bahwa sejumlah "ketidakpastian" atau "risiko" ada dalam banyak akad yang sah secara hukum. Pertanyaan sesungguhnya, sebagaimana ditunjukkan lebih lanjut oleh kutipan Rayner sendiri, adalah dominasi atau pengaruh "ketidakpastian" atau "risiko" tersebut terhadap inti akad. 202

mutlak diperlukan bagi mereka. Jenis risiko ini dianggap tidak dapat diterima dan setara dengan spekulasi karena mengandung ketidakpastian yang inheren. Oleh karena itu, transaksi spekulatif dengan karakteristik ini dilarang…329

Meskipun akad-akad semacam itu dilarang oleh hukum Islam karena sifatnya yang spekulatif atau berisiko — dan karenanya kemungkinan mendapatkan keuntungan dari transaksi tersebut — ia bisa sangat menggoda bagi individu.330 Dengan demikian, Ibnu al-Atsir, kembali pada makna leksikal istilah tersebut, berkata, "Al-Gharar adalah sesuatu yang aspek lahiriahnya lebih disukai, namun aspek batiniahnya tidak disukai oleh orang tersebut. Karenanya, aspek lahiriahnya memikat pembeli sementara aspek batiniahnya tidak diketahui."331

Menurut Ibnu Juzay, contoh-contoh transaksi gharar meliputi:

(1) "Ketidaktahuan tentang harga dan ketidakpastian tentang keberadaan objek."332

(2) "Ketidakpastian tentang harga objek dan karakteristiknya, sebagaimana dalam contoh penjualan kain di toko tanpa spesifikasi apapun tentang kualitas atau harganya."333

(3) "Ketidakpastian yang berkaitan dengan kesulitan penyerahan."334

(4) "Ketidakpastian tentang keberadaan objek, sebagaimana dalam kasus hewan yang sakit."335

329 S. E. Rayner, The Theory of Contracts in Islamic Law (London: Graham & Trotman, 1991), hlm. 289. 330 Kedua pihak harus masuk ke dalam akad atas dasar kehendak bebas. Namun, hal itu tidak berarti bahwa setiap akad yang para pihak bersedia untuk masuki adalah diperbolehkan. 331 Mubarak ibn al-Atsir, Jami' al-Ushul fi Ahadits al-Rasul (Maktabah al-Hilwani, 1971), jil. 1, hlm. 527-528. 332 Nayla Comair-Obeid, The Law of Business Contracts in the Arab Middle East (London: Kluwer Law International, 1996), hlm. 58. 333 Ibid., hlm. 58. 334 Ibid., hlm. 58. 335 Ibid., hlm. 58. 203

Aspek Riba

Salah satu dosa besar yang telah dikenal luas adalah mengambil atau membayar riba.336 Sungguh, setiap Muslim yang mengenal banyak teks yang mengecam riba tidak diragukan lagi akan berusaha sebaik mungkin untuk menghindari segala bentuk riba. Misalnya, Allah berfirman dalam Al-Qur'an, "Orang-orang yang memakan riba tidak dapat berdiri [pada Hari Kiamat] melainkan seperti berdirinya orang yang kemasukan setan lantaran (tekanan) penyakit gila. Keadaan mereka yang demikian itu, adalah disebabkan mereka berkata (berpendapat), sesungguhnya jual beli itu sama dengan riba. Padahal Allah telah menghalalkan jual beli dan mengharamkan riba. Orang-orang yang telah sampai kepadanya larangan dari Tuhannya, lalu terus berhenti (dari mengambil riba), maka baginya apa yang telah diambilnya dahulu (sebelum datang larangan); dan urusannya (terserah) kepada Allah. Orang yang kembali (mengambil riba), maka orang itu adalah penghuni-penghuni neraka; mereka kekal di dalamnya. Allah memusnahkan riba dan menyuburkan sedekah. Dan Allah tidak menyukai setiap orang yang tetap dalam kekafiran, dan selalu berbuat dosa. Sesungguhnya orang-orang yang beriman, mengerjakan amal saleh, mendirikan shalat dan menunaikan zakat, mereka mendapat pahala di sisi Tuhannya. Tidak ada kekhawatiran terhadap mereka dan tidak (pula) mereka bersedih hati. Hai orang-orang yang beriman, bertakwalah kepada Allah dan tinggalkan sisa riba (yang belum dipungut) jika kamu orang-orang yang beriman. Maka jika kamu tidak mengerjakan (meninggalkan sisa riba), maka ketahuilah, bahwa Allah dan Rasul-Nya akan memerangimu. Dan jika kamu bertaubat (dari pengambilan riba), maka bagimu pokok hartamu; kamu tidak menganiaya dan tidak (pula) dianiaya" (al-Baqarah 275-279).

Di antara sekian banyak teks Al-Qur'an dan hadits tentang riba adalah yang berikut ini: Jabir menyatakan, "Rasulullah (shallallahu 'alaihi wa sallam) melaknat pemakan riba, pemberinya, pencatatnya, dan dua saksinya. Mereka semua sama."337 Dalam hadits Nabi (shallallahu 'alaihi wa sallam) yang penting ini, tampak bahwa pemberi dan penerima riba, serta mereka yang membantu dalam akad yang dilarang ini, semuanya sama-sama berdosa dan telah dilaknat oleh Nabi (shallallahu 'alaihi wa sallam).

336 Kata riba terkadang diterjemahkan ke dalam bahasa Inggris dengan sangat kurang tepat sebagai "usury" (rente). "Usury" mengimplikasikan jumlah bunga yang berlebihan, melebihi apa yang diizinkan oleh hukum. Dalam hukum Islam, setiap kelebihan di atas pokok adalah haram. Karenanya, tingkat bunga positif apapun, tidak peduli seberapa kecil, adalah riba dalam hukum Islam. Oleh karena itu, kata "interest" (bunga) adalah terjemahan yang jauh lebih tepat untuk kata riba. 337 Diriwayatkan oleh Muslim. 204

Dengan demikian, diharamkan untuk membayar riba atau menerimanya, dan ini mencakup bunga yang seseorang peroleh dari rekening giro atau tabungannya. Secara umum, jika seseorang ingin meminjamkan uang kepada individu lain yang membutuhkan, tindakan ini seharusnya merupakan tindakan persaudaraan dan kebaikan, dan tidak boleh ada niat untuk meraup keuntungan finansial dalam kasus semacam ini. Jika seseorang ingin melakukan "pinjaman bisnis" dengan harapan meraih keuntungan, maka ia harus bersedia menanggung risikonya dan tidak menuntut imbal hasil yang dijamin atas uangnya. Tidaklah adil bahwa peminjam menanggung risiko dalam usaha bisnisnya sementara pemberi pinjaman tidak menanggung risiko apapun.

Pada saat yang sama, Islam membuka banyak jalan untuk menghindari ketidakadilan sementara investasi tetap berlangsung. Karenanya, perjanjian bagi hasil dalam pinjaman bisnis diperbolehkan, sedangkan pembayaran bunga tetap tidak diperbolehkan.

Penipuan dan kecurangan juga dilarang dalam Islam. Ini mencakup penyembunyian cacat pada barang dagangan atau pekerjaan seseorang secara sengaja. Suatu ketika Nabi (shallallahu 'alaihi wa sallam) sedang berada di pasar dan beliau memasukkan tangannya ke dalam setumpuk biji-bijian, lalu beliau mendapati bahwa bagian bawahnya basah. Beliau bertanya kepada penjual tentang hal itu, dan orang tersebut memberitahunya bahwa hujan telah turun menimpanya. Maka Nabi (shallallahu 'alaihi wa sallam) berkata kepadanya, "Mengapa kamu tidak meletakkannya di atas biji-bijian agar orang-orang dapat melihatnya? Barang siapa menipu, maka ia bukan dari golonganku."338

Ibnu Majah meriwayatkan bahwa Nabi (shallallahu 'alaihi wa sallam) bersabda dalam hadits lain yang menekankan aspek persaudaraan dan kaitannya dengan transaksi bisnis, "Seorang Muslim adalah saudara bagi Muslim lainnya. Tidak halal bagi seorang Muslim untuk menjual sesuatu yang cacat kepada saudaranya tanpa memberitahukan [cacat] tersebut kepadanya."339

Sumber-Sumber Kekayaan Terlarang Lainnya

Ada bentuk-bentuk terlarang lain yang sudah jelas dalam memperoleh kekayaan. Di antaranya adalah perampokan, suap, perjudian, dan transaksi yang sangat spekulatif.338 Diriwayatkan oleh Muslim. 339 Diriwayatkan oleh Ibnu Majah. Ahmad dan al-Hakim memiliki riwayat serupa. Menurut al-Albani, hadits ini sahih. Lihat al-Albani, Shahih al-Jami, jilid 2, hlm. 1136. 205

Seorang Muslim juga tidak diperbolehkan menjual atau memperdagangkan barang-barang yang diharamkan dalam Islam, meskipun ia sendiri tidak mengonsumsinya. Oleh karena itu, seorang Muslim tidak boleh menjual alkohol, pornografi, narkoba, dan sejenisnya.

Dalam bab ini, telah dibahas interaksi seorang mukmin. Keimanan yang sejati kepada Allah seharusnya meresapi setiap aspek kehidupan seseorang, terutama dalam interaksinya dengan sesama makhluk Allah. Dengan demikian, dimulai dari cara ia memperlakukan dirinya sendiri, seseorang menyadari bahwa ia adalah salah satu makhluk Allah dan oleh karenanya harus memperlakukan dirinya sendiri dengan cara yang diridhai Allah. Untuk mencapai tujuan ini, Allah dengan penuh kasih sayang telah mengutus manusia petunjuk yang jelas untuk mengajarkan mereka cara berperilaku bahkan terhadap diri mereka sendiri.

Individu yang diciptakan oleh Allah jelas tidak dapat mengklaim bagi dirinya hak untuk memperlakukan orang lain sesuka hatinya. Sekali lagi, Allah telah memberikan petunjuk dalam bidang ini pula. Baik dalam hubungannya dengan orang tua, anak-anak, tetangga, maupun anggota masyarakat lainnya—bahkan terhadap hewan dan benda-benda tidak bernyawa seperti harta benda—terdapat perilaku yang mencerminkan keimanan seseorang kepada Allah. Sudah sepatutnya seorang mukmin mempelajari perilaku yang benar ini dan berusaha menjalaninya dalam kehidupannya semaksimal mungkin.

Mempertebal dan Meningkatkan Keimanan

Bab ini membahas cara-cara mempertebal dan meningkatkan keimanan. Sebagaimana telah disinggung sebelumnya, seorang Muslim tidak boleh merasa puas dan lengah terhadap keimanannya. Iman dapat bertambah dan berkurang. Rasulullah (shallallahu 'alaihi wa sallam) bersabda, "Demi Dzat yang tiada tuhan selain-Nya, salah seorang di antara kalian melakukan amalan ahli surga hingga hanya tersisa jarak sejengkal antara dirinya dan surga, namun kemudian takdir (yang telah ditetapkan) mendahuluinya dan ia pun melakukan amalan ahli neraka sehingga ia masuk ke dalamnya."340

Seorang Muslim harus sangat jelas mengenai tujuan hidupnya. Ketika ia sudah jelas tentang tujuan hidupnya, ia harus mampu mengidentifikasi sarana dan langkah-langkah yang akan membantunya mencapai tujuan tersebut. Demikian pula, ia harus menyadari hambatan dan hal-hal berbahaya yang merugikannya dalam mencapai tujuannya. Terakhir, ketika ia tergelincir, ia perlu mengetahui cara terbaik yang akan mengembalikannya—dengan izin Allah—ke jalan yang benar.
title_id: Terjemahan Part 40 selesai dan disimpan ke `guide-for-new-muslim/040.json` dalam field `content_id`.
content_ar:
Another general principle with respect to business transactions is that they are permissible unless there is evidence to demonstrate that they are forbidden; only if it is found that it contains some forbidden aspect will it be deemed forbidden. Thus, Islamic Law has laid down some principle guidelines while delineating particular forbidden aspects that must be avoided. The matters that should be avoided include unstated or undetermined terms, speculative or overly risky conditions, interest, gambling and fraud or deception. If any one of these factors is found in a contract, the contract, depending on the extent to which they are present, may be rendered null, void and impermissible. It is essential that Muslims be aware of these forbidden characteristics so

325 Recorded by al-Bukhari and Muslim. 326 Recorded by al-Bukhari and Muslim. 201

that they may live off of pure and permissible sources. Thus, some of them are discussed in some detail below.

Gharar refers to speculative or overly risky transactions. Imam Muslim records in his Sahih: "On the authority of Abu Hurairah who said that رسول الله ﷺ (peace and blessings of الله be upon him) forbade 'sales of speculative nature' (bai' al-gharar)." Al-Bukhari and Muslim record: "On the authority of ibn Umar who said that 'رسول الله ﷺ (peace and blessings of الله be upon him) prohibited the sale of fruits until their ripeness and freedom from disease were apparent. He prohibited both the seller and the buyer.'" Commenting on a الحديث with similar meaning, al-Nawawi explained why the prohibition was for both the seller and the buyer. He wrote, "As for the seller, it is because he is wanting to devour wealth wrongfully. As for the buyer, it is because he is in accord with him on this forbidden act and because he is [possibly] wasting his wealth while wasting wealth has been prohibited."327

From these الحديث and others, there is a consensus among jurists that an overwhelming presence of undue risk or uncertainty renders a business contract null and void. Such transactions are ones in which the probability of one or both of the parties being wronged is great. Concerning the meaning of this concept of gharar, Rayner states,

The Sharee'ah determined that in the interests of fair, ethical dealing in commutative contracts, unjustified enrichment should be prohibited. This policy precludes any element of uncertainty or risk (Gharar).328 In a general context, the unanimous proposition of the jurists held that in any transaction, by failing or neglecting to define any of the essential pillars of contract relating to the consideration or the object, the parties undertake a risk which is not indis-

327 Yahya al-Nawawi, al-Minhaaj Sharh sahih Muslim ibn a-Hajjaaj (Beirut: Daar al-Ma'rifah, 1999), vol. 10, p. 424. 328 The statement, "any element of uncertainty or risk," is not quite correct. The Muslim jurists have long recognized that amounts of "uncertainty" or "risk" exist in many legal contracts. The true question, as much of Rayner's quote itself further shows, is the predominance or the effect of such "uncertainty" or "risk" on the essence of the contract. 202

pensable for them. This kind of risk was deemed unacceptable and tantamount to speculation due to its inherent uncertainty. Speculative transactions with these characteristics are therefore prohibited…329

Although such contracts are prohibited by Islamic Law, due to their speculative or risky nature and hence the possibility of making gains from such transactions, they can be very alluring to individuals.330 Thus, ibn al-Atheer, going back to the lexical meaning of the term, says, "Al-Gharar is that concerning which its apparent component is preferable but its non-apparent component is disliked to the person. Hence, its apparent component entices the buyer while its non-apparent component is unknown."331

According to ibn Juzay, examples of gharar transactions include:

(1) "Ignorance of the price and uncertainty about the existence of the object."332

(2) "Uncertainty about the price of the object and about its characteristics, as in the example of the sale of cloth in a shop without any specification about its quality or price."333

(3) "Uncertainty related to difficulties of delivery."334

(4) "Uncertainty about the existence of the object, as in the case of a sickly animal."335

329 S. E. Rayner, The Theory of Contracts in Islamic Law (London: Graham & Trotman, 1991), p. 289. 330 Both parties must enter into contacts out of free will. However, that does not mean that every contract in which parties are willing to enter is permissible. 331 Mubaarak ibn al-Atheer, Jaami al-Usool fi Ahaadeeth al-Rasool (Maktaba al-Hilwaani. 1971), vol. 1, pp. 527-528. 332 Nayla Comair-Obeid, The Law of Business Contracts in the Arab Middle East (London: Kluwer Law Interna onal, 1996), p. 58. 333 Ibid., p. 58. 334 Ibid., p. 58. 335 Ibid., p. 58. 203

The Aspect of Riba (Interest)

One of the well-known great sins is the taking or paying of riba (inter336 est). Indeed, any Muslim familiar with the numerous texts censuring riba would undoubtedly do his best to avoid any trace of riba. For example, الله has said in the القرآن الكريم, "Those who devour interest will not stand [on the Day of Judgment] save as he arises whom the devil has deranged by (his) touch. That is because they say, 'Trade is just like interest,' whereas الله has permitted trading and has forbidden interest. He unto whom an admonition from his Lord comes, and (he) refrains (in obedience thereto), shall keep [the money of] that which is past, and his affair (henceforth) is with الله. As for him who returns (to interest), such are rightful owners of the Fire. They will abide therein forever. الله destroys interest and gives an increase for charity. الله loves not every disbelieving, sinner. Truly, [as for] those who believe, perform righteous deeds, establish the prayer and pay the zakah, their reward is with their Lord. No fear shall come upon them neither shall they grieve. O you who believe! Observe your duty to الله, and give up what remains (due to you) in interest, if you are (in truth) believers. And if you do not, then be informed of a war from الله and His messenger. But if you repent, then you have your principal [without interest]. Do not wrong [others] and you shall not be wronged" (al-Baqarah 275-279).

Among the other numerous Quranic and الحديث texts concerning interest is the following: Jaabir stated, "رسول الله ﷺ (peace and blessings of الله be upon him) cursed the taker of interest, its giver, its recorder and its two witnesses. They are all alike."337 In this important الحديث of النبي ﷺ (peace and blessings of الله be upon him) one sees that the giver and the receiver as well as those who assisted in this forbidden contract are all equally sinful and have all been cursed by النبي ﷺ (peace and blessings of الله be upon him).

336 The word riba is sometimes very poorly and improperly translated into English as "usury." "Usury" implies an exorbitant amount of interest, above and beyond what is permissible by law. In Islamic law, any increase above the principle is forbidden. Hence, any positive rate of interest, no matter how low, is both interest and usury in Islamic law. Hence, the word interest is a much better translation for the word riba. 337 Recorded by Muslim. 204

Thus, it is forbidden to pay interest or to receive interest, and this includes the interest that one earns on one's checking or savings account. In general, if an individual wishes to loan money to another individual who is in need, this act should be a brotherly and charitable act and there should be no thought of reaping monetary benefits in such a case. If someone wishes to make a "business loan," hoping to reap profits, then he should be willing to put his risk and not demand a guaranteed return for his money. It is not fair that the borrower is at risk in his business venture while the lender faces no risk at all.

At the same time, Islam opens the door to many avenues by which injustice is avoided while investment still takes place. Hence, profit-sharing agreements on business loans are allowed while fixed interest payments are not.

Fraud and deception are also forbidden in Islam. This would include intentionally concealing defects in one's merchandise or work. One time النبي ﷺ (صلى الله عليه وسلم) was in the marketplace and he put his hand into a pile of grains and he found that it was wet on the bottom. He asked the vendor about it and the man told him that rain had fallen upon it. So النبي ﷺ (صلى الله عليه وسلم) told him, "Why did you not put it on top of the grain so people could see it? Whoever deceives is not from me."338

Ibn Maajah records that النبي ﷺ (صلى الله عليه وسلم) said in another الحديث stressing the aspect of brotherhood and its relation to business dealings, "A Muslim is a brother to a Muslim. It is not permissible for a Muslim to sell something defective to his brother without making that [defect] clear to him."339

Other Forbidden Sources of Wealth

There are other obvious forbidden forms of obtaining wealth. These include robbery, through bribery, gambling and extremely speculative transac338 Recorded by Muslim. 339 Recorded by ibn Maajah. Ahmad and al-Haakim have something similar. According to alAlbaani, it is sahih. See al-Albaani, sahih al-Jaami, vol. 2, p. 1136. 205

tions. A Muslim is also not allowed to sell or trade articles that are forbidden in Islam, even if he himself does not consume them. Hence, a Muslim is not allowed to sell alcohol, porn, drugs and the like.

In this chapter, the interactions of a believer were discussed. The true belief in God should permeate every aspect of an individual's life, especially his interaction with others of God's creation. Thus, starting with how he treats himself, the individual realizes that he is one of God's creatures and therefore must treat himself in a way that is pleasing to God. In order to achieve this goal, الله has mercifully sent humans clear guidance to teach them how to behave even with respect to their own selves.

The individual created by God obviously, then, cannot claim for himself the right to treat others in any way he wishes. Again, الله has provided guidance in this area as well. Whether it is with respect to one's parents, children, neighbors, others in society-even animals and inanimate objects such as wealth-there is a way of behavior that is representative of one's belief in الله. It behooves a believer to learn this proper behavior and to exert himself to live by it in his life to the best of his ability.

Fortifying and Increasing One's Faith

This chapter is a discussion of the means of fortifying and increasing one's faith. As has been alluded to earlier, a Muslim should never be complacent with respect to his faith. Faith increases and decreases. رسول الله ﷺ (peace and blessings of الله be upon him) said, "By the One whom there is no other God, one of you does the actions of Paradise until there is just a hand's span between him and Paradise and then the Book [preordainment] overtakes him and he does the actions of the people of Hell and he enters into it."340

A Muslim must be very clear about his goal in life. When he is clear about his goal in life, he must be able to identify the means and measures that will assist him to meet that goal. Similarly, he must be aware of the impediments and harmful aspects that harm him with respect to his goal. Finally, when he does slip, he needs to know the best way that will put him, الله willing, right back on the proper track.
